concepts.title: Line Touching of two circle  Part 1
concepts.conceptBody:
Contact of Two Circles 

Working Rule: 

The two circles having centers at $A\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}} \right)$ and $B\left( {{x}_{2}},{{y}_{2}} \right)$ and radii ${{r}_{1}}$ and ${{r}_{2}}$ respectively touch each other.

${{\text{r}}_{1}}-{{\text{r}}_{2}}=\text{AB}+\text{DC}$ AB = ${{r}_{1}}-{{r}_{2}}$ ${{r}_{1}}+{{r}_{2}}=AC+CB+DC$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ AB}<{{\text{r}}_{1}}-{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ $=AB+DC$

$\therefore AB<{{r}_{1}}+{{r}_{2}}$

$\text{AB}={{\text{r}}_{1}}+{{\text{r}}_{2}}$+ CD

$\text{AB}={{\text{r}}_{1}}+{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ AB > ${{r}_{1}}+{{r}_{2}}$

concepts.title: Intersection of Line and Circle
concepts.conceptBody:
Intersection of a line & a circle 

Any line will cut a circle either 
(a) at two distinct points or 
(b) at two coincident points or 
(c) at two imaginary points.

In the second case the line will be tangent to the circle. Let us observe the intersection of the line $y=mx+c$ and the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$. The x-ordinates of points of intersection of the line and the circle are given by the equation

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{(\text{mx}+\text{c})}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}+2\text{mcx}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}-{{\text{a}}^{2}}=0 \\ \end{array}$

In general, there are 3 cases arises. When circle and a line intersect

Case I: 

There are two different real points of intersection.

\(\begin{aligned} & \text {i.e., } \Delta>0 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{c}^2-4\left(1+\mathrm{m}^2\right)\left(\mathrm{c}^2-\mathrm{a}^2\right)>0 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{a}^2\left(1+\mathrm{m}^2\right)>\mathrm{c}^2 \\ & \text { or } a >\left|\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{m}^2}}\right|\end{aligned}\)

Case II: 

There are two coincident points of intersection.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }=0 \\ {} & 4\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{2}}{{\text{c}}^{2}}-4\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)\left( {{\text{c}}^{2}}-{{\text{a}}^{2}} \right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & {{\text{a}}^{2}}\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)={{\text{c}}^{2}} \\ \Rightarrow & \text{a}=\left| \frac{\text{c}}{\sqrt{1+{{\text{m}}^{2}}}} \right| \\ \Rightarrow & \text{c}=\pm \text{a}\sqrt{1+{{\text{m}}^{2}}} \\ \end{array}$

This is the required condition of tangency.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ the equation the tangents are $\text{y}=\text{mx}+\text{a}\sqrt{1+{{\text{m}}^{2}}}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=mx-a\sqrt{1+{{m}^{2}}}$

Case III: 

There is no real point of intersection

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}{} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }<0 \\{} & ~\Rightarrow 4\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{2}}{{\text{c}}^{2}}-4\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)\left( {{\text{c}}^{2}}-{{\text{a}}^{2}} \right)<0 \\{} & ~\Rightarrow {{\text{m}}^{2}}{{\text{c}}^{2}}-\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)\left( {{\text{c}}^{2}}-{{\text{a}}^{2}} \right)<0 \\{} & ~\Rightarrow {{\text{m}}^{2}}{{\text{c}}^{2}}-{{\text{c}}^{2}}-{{\text{m}}^{2}}{{\text{c}}^{2}}+{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{a}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{2}}<0 \\{} & ~\Rightarrow {{\text{a}}^{2}}\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)<{{\text{c}}^{2}} \\{} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{a}}^{2}}<\frac{{{\text{c}}^{2}}}{\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)} \\{} & ~\Rightarrow \text{a}<\left| \frac{\text{c}}{\sqrt{1+{{\text{m}}^{2}}}} \right| \\ \end{array}$

Thus, the line $\text{y}=\text{mx}+\text{c}$; will

(i) cut the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ at two distinct points if ${{a}^{2}}\left( 1+{{m}^{2}} \right)>{{c}^{2}}$

(ii) touch the circle if, ${{\text{a}}^{2}}\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)={{\text{c}}^{2}}$

(iii) not cut the circle at all if ${{\text{a}}^{2}}\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)<{{\text{c}}^{2}}$.

Note: 

(1) The line $y=mx\pm a\sqrt{1+{{m}^{2}}}$ will be a tangent to the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ for all values of $m$.

(2) The condition of tangency can also be obtained by equating perpendicular distance of the Centre $\left( 0,0 \right)$ than the line $\text{y}-\text{mx}-\text{c}=0$ to the radius of the circle.

(3) In case the circle is ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$ we may shift the origin to the Centre of the circle and apply the results described above.

 

Another Method: 

Let $S={{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$ and $L=1x+my+n=0$

Let $r$ be the radius of the circle and $p$ be the length of the perpendicular drawn from the Centre $\left( -g,-f \right)$ on the line L. Then

(i) line intersect the circle in two distinct points if $p<r$.

(ii) line touch the circle if $\text{p}=\text{r}$

(iii) line neither intersects nor touches the circle i.e., passes outside the circle if $\text{p}>\text{r}$.

concepts.title: Line Touching of two circle  Part 2
concepts.conceptBody:
Length of the chord 

Let $O$ be the Centre of the circle $S=0$ and the line of the chord $AB$ be $\text{L}=0$

Let $\text{p}=$ length of $\bot $ from Centre to the line $\text{L}=0$

Then $\text{AB}=$ length of the chord $=2\sqrt{\left( {{\text{r}}^{2}}-{{\text{p}}^{2}} \right)}$. (by Pythagoras theorem)

examples.title: For the line $3 x+2 y=12$ and the circle $x^2+y^2-4 x-6 y+3=0$, which of the following statements is true

If the straight line $y=m x$ is outside the circle $x^2+y^2-20 y+90=0$, then
